name: Iceland
tagline: From the Landnám and the world's oldest parliament to a volcanic republic that won its seas.
summary: Iceland's history begins late and in fire. Settled by Norse and Celtic newcomers in the Viking Age, conventionally from 874, this empty north-Atlantic island filled within sixty years and in 930 founded the Althing at Þingvellir, one of the oldest parliaments in the world and the assembly of a stateless Commonwealth that governed without a king. Around the year 1000 the same assembly voted, peacefully, to accept Christianity, and in the thirteenth century Icelanders wrote the sagas and Eddas that preserved the literature of the Norse world. Civil strife in the Age of the Sturlungs ended that independence: by the Old Covenant of 1262-64 Iceland submitted to the Norwegian crown, and with Norway it passed to Denmark, enduring the imposed Reformation, a Danish trade monopoly and the catastrophe of the Laki eruption of 1783-84. A nineteenth-century awakening led by Jón Sigurðsson restored the Althing and won Home Rule in 1904 and sovereignty in 1918; on 17 June 1944, with Denmark under German occupation, Icelanders proclaimed a republic at Þingvellir. The modern republic became a founding member of NATO in 1949, fought and won the Cod Wars over its fishing seas, helped establish the 200-mile exclusive economic zone in international law, and survived a spectacular banking collapse in 2008, all upon a land split by the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.

founding.label: The founding of the Republic at Þingvellir
founding.detail: Iceland's modern statehood is dated to 17 June 1944, when, in a ceremony at the ancient assembly plain of Þingvellir, Icelanders proclaimed a republic and dissolved the personal union with the Danish crown. The act followed an overwhelming referendum, in which more than 95 percent of voters backed the republic, and was timed to the lapse of the 1918 Act of Union while Denmark itself lay under German occupation. Sveinn Björnsson became the first president. The date was deliberately symbolic: Þingvellir had been the seat of the Althing since 930, and the republic was understood as the restoration of an independence that Icelanders traced back to the Commonwealth and that had been lost to the Norwegian crown by the Old Covenant of 1262-64. 17 June remains Iceland's national day.

quickFacts.label: Settlement (Landnám)
quickFacts.value: Norse and Celtic settlers from c. 874; Ingólfr Arnarson the first permanent settler, at Reykjavík

quickFacts.label: Oldest parliament
quickFacts.value: The Althing (Alþingi) founded at Þingvellir in 930, an assembly of the Icelandic Commonwealth

quickFacts.label: Conversion to Christianity
quickFacts.value: Adopted by vote of the Althing c. 1000, peacefully, through the lawspeaker Þorgeir

quickFacts.label: End of independence
quickFacts.value: Old Covenant (Gamli sáttmáli), 1262-64: submission to the Norwegian crown

quickFacts.label: Republic founded
quickFacts.value: Republic proclaimed at Þingvellir, 17 June 1944, ending union with Denmark

quickFacts.label: Cod Wars
quickFacts.value: Fishing limits extended to 12, 50 and 200 nautical miles (1958, 1972, 1975), helping create the 200-mile EEZ

eras.title: The Settlement and the Icelandic Commonwealth
eras.standfirst: Norse and Celtic newcomers filled an empty island within two generations and, having no king, built a free state governed by an assembly at Þingvellir.
body: Iceland was among the last large habitable lands of Europe to be settled. According to Íslendingabók, the Book of the Icelanders, written about 1130, and the Landnámabók, the Book of Settlements, the country was discovered and settled by Norse people in the Viking Age, with the age of settlement, the Landnám, falling roughly between 870 and 930. Tradition names Ingólfr Arnarson as the first permanent settler, who came from Norway around 874 and made his homestead at a site he called Reykjavík, the 'smoky bay'. The Book of Settlements goes on to enumerate hundreds of settlers who came with their families, servants and slaves; most sailed from Norway, but many came by way of the Norse colonies in the British Isles, bringing a Celtic strain into the new society. Both sources record that before them Irish monks, the Papar, had lived on the island and departed.

Within about sixty years the habitable coasts and valleys had been claimed, and a society of independent farmers and chieftains, the goðar, had taken shape. It had no king and no central executive. To resolve the disputes such a society inevitably generated, the settlers created, around the year 930, a general assembly, the Althing (Alþingi), which met each summer on the lava plain of Þingvellir, the 'assembly plains', some forty-five kilometres from Reykjavík. There the lawspeaker recited the law from the Lögberg, the Law Rock, holding the whole code in memory and reciting it in thirds over three years, while the legislative council, the Lögrétta, made and amended the laws. The Althing of 930 is reckoned one of the oldest functioning legislatures in the world.

This was the Icelandic Commonwealth, or Free State, a polity that endured for more than three centuries on consensus and law rather than monarchy. Þingvellir during the assembly became a kind of summer capital, a centre of social and commercial life that drew labourers, traders and even the envoys of foreign princes. The arrangement was distinctive in medieval Europe: power lay with the chieftains and the assembly, and the law was the highest authority. It produced both a remarkable legal culture and, in time, the literature that would make Iceland's medieval centuries famous.

events.title: Ingólfr Arnarson settles Reykjavík
events.summary: By tradition the first permanent settler, Ingólfr Arnarson, came from Norway around 874 and established his homestead at the site he named Reykjavík, opening the age of settlement, the Landnám.

events.title: The Landnám (Age of Settlement)
events.summary: Within roughly sixty years hundreds of Norse settlers, many by way of the Norse colonies in the British Isles, claimed Iceland's habitable land, as recorded in Íslendingabók and Landnámabók.

events.title: Founding of the Althing at Þingvellir
events.summary: The settlers established a general assembly, the Althing, on the plain of Þingvellir, where law was recited from the Law Rock; it is one of the oldest parliaments in the world and the centre of the Icelandic Commonwealth.

events.title: The Icelandic Commonwealth
events.summary: A stateless society of chieftains and farmers governed itself for over three centuries through the Althing and its law council, without a king or central executive.

figures.name: Ingólfr Arnarson
figures.blurb: Norse settler who, by the testimony of the Landnámabók, came from Norway around 874 and made the first lasting home in Iceland at Reykjavík, the site of the future capital. He stands as the symbolic founder of the Icelandic nation.

figures.name: Þorgeir Ljósvetningagoði
figures.blurb: Lawspeaker who, at the Althing around the year 1000, was entrusted to decide between pagans and Christians and ruled that Iceland should accept Christianity, preserving the unity of the Commonwealth without bloodshed.

pullquote.text: About 874 the settling of Iceland began in earnest, and in 930 the islanders established what remains one of the oldest parliamentary assemblies in the world.
pullquote.attribution: On the Landnám and the founding of the Althing

eras.title: Christianity, the Sagas and the Old Covenant
eras.standfirst: The Althing voted itself Christian without civil war, the chieftains wrote the sagas and Eddas, and then internal feud delivered the free state into the hands of the Norwegian crown.
body: Around the year 1000 the Althing faced a rupture that elsewhere in Europe would have meant war. Missionaries, backed by the Norwegian king Olaf Tryggvason, had won converts, and the assembly split into pagan and Christian parties, each threatening to live under its own law. The dispute was referred to the lawspeaker, Þorgeir Ljósvetningagoði, himself a pagan, who withdrew for a day and a night and then announced from the Law Rock that, to keep one law and one people, Iceland should adopt Christianity, while older private practices might continue for a time. The conversion was thus achieved by compromise and vote rather than by the sword, an episode Icelanders have remembered ever since as a triumph of law over faction.

Christianity brought writing, and writing made the Icelandic centuries that followed extraordinary. In the twelfth and thirteenth centuries Icelanders produced a body of vernacular literature unmatched in medieval Europe: the family sagas, the kings' sagas, and the mythological and poetic learning of the Eddas. The towering figure was Snorri Sturluson (1179-1241), chieftain, twice lawspeaker of the Althing, and author of the Prose Edda, a handbook of poetics that preserves the legends of Norse mythology, and the Heimskringla, a history of the kings of Norway. Through Snorri and his contemporaries Iceland became the keeper of the Norse world's memory.

Snorri's own violent death foreshadowed the end of the Commonwealth. From the 1220s the concentration of power in a few great families, the Sturlungs chief among them, plunged Iceland into the Age of the Sturlungs, decades of feud, raids, killings and burnings in which chieftains intrigued at the Norwegian court for advantage. Exhausted by strife and pressed by King Haakon IV of Norway, the Icelandic chieftains accepted the Old Covenant, the Gamli sáttmáli, between 1262 and 1264, swearing fidelity to the Norwegian crown. After more than three centuries the free state was over, and Iceland had a king for the first time.

events.title: The Althing adopts Christianity
events.summary: Faced with a split between pagans and Christians, the Althing entrusted the decision to the lawspeaker Þorgeir, who ruled that Iceland should become Christian; the conversion was achieved by compromise, without bloodshed.

events.title: Snorri Sturluson and the sagas
events.summary: Chieftain and twice lawspeaker, Snorri Sturluson wrote the Prose Edda and the Heimskringla, central works of the saga age that preserved the mythology and history of the Norse world.

events.title: The Age of the Sturlungs
events.summary: Rivalry among a few powerful families, above all the Sturlungs, brought decades of feud, killing and burning, fatally weakening the Commonwealth's institutions of consensus.

events.title: The Old Covenant (Gamli sáttmáli)
events.summary: Worn down by civil strife and pressed by King Haakon IV, the Icelandic chieftains swore allegiance to the Norwegian crown, ending the independent Commonwealth and beginning centuries of foreign rule.

figures.name: Snorri Sturluson
figures.blurb: Born at Hvammur and twice lawspeaker of the Althing, Snorri was the greatest writer of the Icelandic medieval world. His Prose Edda and Heimskringla preserve the mythology and the royal history of Norse Scandinavia. He was killed in 1241 amid the feuds of the Sturlung Age.

figures.name: Haakon IV of Norway
figures.blurb: The Norwegian king whose persistent pressure and exploitation of Icelandic feuds brought the chieftains to accept his sovereignty in the Old Covenant of 1262-64, ending the Icelandic Commonwealth.

pullquote.text: Christianity started making inroads into Icelandic society without resort to weapons or bloodshed, decided by the lawspeaker from the Law Rock around the year 1000.
pullquote.attribution: Þingvellir National Park, on the conversion

eras.title: Under Norway and Denmark: Reformation, Monopoly and Famine
eras.standfirst: Passing with Norway under the Danish crown, Iceland endured an imposed Reformation, a crushing trade monopoly and recurring natural disaster on a volcanic land.
body: After 1262 Iceland was governed for the Norwegian king, and when Norway, Denmark and Sweden were joined in the Kalmar Union and the Scandinavian crowns consolidated, Iceland passed under the Danish crown, where it would remain for centuries. Distant and poor, dependent on the sea and on a few exportable goods, the island had little leverage over the policies decided in Copenhagen. The medieval Althing continued to meet at Þingvellir, but real power lay with the crown and its officials.

The sixteenth century brought the Reformation, imposed from above. King Christian III of Denmark moved to make all his subjects Lutheran, and Iceland's Catholic establishment resisted. Jón Arason, bishop of Hólar and the last Catholic bishop in Iceland, led the opposition until he and his two sons were beheaded at Skálholt on 7 November 1550; with their deaths the Catholic Church in Iceland effectively came to an end and the country became Lutheran. In 1602 the crown imposed a trade monopoly that confined all Icelandic commerce to a small group of Danish merchants operating from licensed posts. The monopoly, which lasted until 1786, kept prices high, returns low and the island chronically short of goods, and is remembered as a long economic stranglehold.

Above all, Iceland's history under Denmark was shaped by the violence of its own land, which sits astride the Mid-Atlantic Ridge where the North American and Eurasian plates pull apart. The greatest of its disasters began on 8 June 1783, when the Laki (Lakagígar) fissure erupted, pouring out some 14.7 cubic kilometres of lava over eight months and releasing vast clouds of poisonous gas. Fluorine-laden fallout killed the majority of the country's livestock, and the famine that followed, the Móðuharðindin or 'famine of the mist', is reckoned to have killed roughly a fifth of the Icelandic population. The eruption's haze spread across Europe and beyond, and it stands as the worst catastrophe in Icelandic history.

events.title: Execution of Bishop Jón Arason
events.summary: Jón Arason, bishop of Hólar and the last Catholic bishop in Iceland, was beheaded with his two sons at Skálholt for resisting the Danish-imposed Reformation, after which Iceland became fully Lutheran.

events.title: The Danish trade monopoly
events.summary: The Danish crown confined all Icelandic trade to a small group of licensed Danish merchants, depressing prices and returns and impoverishing the island for nearly two centuries.

events.title: The Laki eruption
events.summary: The Laki fissure erupted for eight months from June 1783, producing about 14.7 cubic kilometres of lava and immense quantities of toxic gas that poisoned the land and spread a haze across Europe.

events.title: The Móðuharðindin famine
events.summary: Fallout from Laki killed most of Iceland's livestock; the ensuing 'famine of the mist' killed roughly a fifth of the population, the worst disaster in the country's history.

figures.name: Jón Arason
figures.blurb: Bishop of Hólar and a noted poet, Jón Arason resisted the Reformation imposed by Christian III of Denmark. His execution with his sons at Skálholt in 1550 ended Catholic Iceland and made him a national as well as a religious hero.

figures.name: Christian III of Denmark
figures.blurb: The Danish king who imposed Lutheranism across his realms, including Iceland, breaking the power of the Catholic bishops and bringing the island into the Protestant world.

pullquote.text: The famine that followed the Laki eruption, the Móðuharðindin, killed roughly a fifth of Iceland's people and remains the worst disaster in the nation's history.

eras.title: National Awakening, Home Rule and Sovereignty
eras.standfirst: A nineteenth-century revival, led by Jón Sigurðsson and rooted in the memory of the Commonwealth, restored the Althing and carried Iceland to Home Rule and then to sovereign statehood.
body: The recovery from the famines and monopoly of the eighteenth century coincided with a wave of national feeling that swept Europe in the nineteenth. In Iceland it drew strength from the sagas and from the memory of the Commonwealth and the Althing, and it found its leader in Jón Sigurðsson (1811-1879), a scholar in Copenhagen who became the tireless advocate of Icelandic rights, pressing for the abolition of the trade monopoly and for self-government. A royal decree of 1843 restored the Althing, which first met again on 1 July 1845, at first only as an advisory body to the Danish king. Þingvellir, the old assembly plain, became a powerful symbol of national unity, the site of nationalist gatherings from 1848 onward.

Progress came in measured steps. In 1874, on the thousandth anniversary of the settlement, Denmark granted Iceland a constitution and a measure of control over its own affairs. In 1904 Home Rule was established, with an Icelandic minister resident in Reykjavík responsible to the Althing rather than to Copenhagen, a decisive advance in self-government. The decades around the turn of the century also transformed Icelandic society, as decked fishing vessels, the growth of Reykjavík and new institutions modernised a country that had long been among the poorest in Europe.

The First World War and its aftermath opened the way to sovereignty. On 1 December 1918 the Danish-Icelandic Act of Union made Iceland a free and sovereign state, the Kingdom of Iceland, joined to Denmark only in a personal union under a shared king, with Denmark still conducting Iceland's foreign affairs. The Act was to be reviewed after twenty-five years and could then be terminated, a clause that would prove decisive a generation later. For the first time since 1262, Iceland was a sovereign state in its own right.

events.title: The Althing restored
events.summary: A royal decree of 1843 revived the Althing, which first met again on 1 July 1845 as an advisory assembly, reviving in modern form the institution founded in 930.

events.title: Constitution on the settlement millennium
events.summary: On the thousandth anniversary of the settlement, Denmark granted Iceland a constitution and increased control over domestic affairs, though executive power remained with the crown.

events.title: Home Rule
events.summary: Iceland gained Home Rule, with an Icelandic minister resident in Reykjavík and answerable to the Althing, a major step toward full self-government.

events.title: The Act of Union and the Kingdom of Iceland
events.summary: The Danish-Icelandic Act of Union made Iceland a sovereign state in personal union with the Danish crown, with Denmark retaining foreign affairs and the union open to termination after twenty-five years.

figures.name: Jón Sigurðsson
figures.blurb: Scholar and statesman, the central figure of Iceland's nineteenth-century awakening, who campaigned for the end of the Danish trade monopoly and for self-government. He is Iceland's national hero, and his birthday, 17 June, was chosen as the date for founding the republic.

figures.name: Hannes Hafstein
figures.role: First minister for Iceland under Home Rule
figures.blurb: Poet and statesman who in 1904 became the first Icelandic minister under Home Rule, resident in Reykjavík and responsible to the Althing, presiding over a period of rapid modernisation.

pullquote.text: On 1 December 1918 the Act of Union made Iceland a free and sovereign state in personal union with Denmark.
pullquote.attribution: On the Danish-Icelandic Act of Union

eras.title: Occupation, the Republic and NATO
eras.standfirst: Wartime occupation by Britain and the United States severed the link to an occupied Denmark; Icelanders proclaimed a republic at Þingvellir in 1944 and abandoned neutrality to help found NATO.
body: Iceland's sovereignty under the 1918 union was barely two decades old when the Second World War transformed its position. Germany occupied Denmark in April 1940, cutting Iceland off from its monarch and the conduct of its own foreign affairs. The strategically placed island, commanding the North Atlantic sea lanes, could not remain untouched: British forces occupied Iceland in May 1940, and from 1941 the United States, by agreement with the Icelandic government, took over its defence, building up a major presence. Occupation brought hardship but also employment and money, accelerating the modernisation of the country.

With Denmark under German rule and the Act of Union due for review after twenty-five years, Icelanders moved to complete their independence. A referendum in 1944 produced overwhelming majorities to dissolve the union and establish a republic. On 17 June 1944, at Þingvellir, the seat of the Althing since 930, the Republic of Iceland was proclaimed and the personal union with the Danish crown was ended. Sveinn Björnsson became the first president. The date, the birthday of Jón Sigurðsson, fused the ancient assembly, the nineteenth-century awakening and the new state into a single national symbol.

The new republic quickly abandoned its long tradition of neutrality. Recognising its strategic exposure, Iceland joined the United Nations and, in 1949, became a founding member of the North Atlantic Treaty Organisation, though as a country with no armed forces of its own and on the understanding that it would not be asked to host foreign troops in peacetime. The decision was bitterly contested at home, marked by a communist-led riot during the parliamentary debate, but it was carried by a substantial majority and set the course of Icelandic security policy for the Cold War.

events.title: British occupation of Iceland
events.summary: After Germany occupied Denmark, British forces occupied strategically vital Iceland in May 1940 to forestall a German move, severing Iceland's last constitutional ties to its monarch.

events.title: United States assumes Iceland's defence
events.summary: By agreement with the Icelandic government, the United States took over the island's defence in 1941, building a substantial military presence in the North Atlantic.

events.title: Founding of the Republic at Þingvellir
events.summary: After an overwhelming referendum, Icelanders proclaimed a republic at Þingvellir, ending the union with Denmark; Sveinn Björnsson became the first president and 17 June became the national day.

events.title: Iceland a founding member of NATO
events.summary: Abandoning neutrality, Iceland joined NATO as a founding member in 1949, a state with no army, on the understanding it would not host foreign troops in peacetime; the decision provoked a riot at the Althing.

figures.name: Sveinn Björnsson
figures.role: First President of the Republic of Iceland
figures.blurb: Diplomat and statesman who served as regent during the war and, on 17 June 1944, became the first president of the new republic, a post he held until his death in 1952.

figures.name: Ólafur Thors
figures.role: Prime Minister at the founding of the republic
figures.blurb: Leader of the Independence Party and several times prime minister, Ólafur Thors headed the government that proclaimed the republic in 1944 and led the contested decision to join NATO in 1949.

pullquote.text: On 17 June 1944 a gathering at Þingvellir proclaimed the Republic of Iceland, ending the union with Denmark on the very plain where the Althing had met since 930.
pullquote.attribution: On the founding of the republic

eras.title: The Cod Wars and the Modern Republic
eras.standfirst: A small, unarmed state defeated the Royal Navy at sea, won control of its fishing grounds and helped write the 200-mile EEZ into international law, then weathered the largest banking collapse in history.
body: For a nation whose wealth came from the sea, control of the surrounding fishing grounds was a matter of survival, and it brought Iceland into a remarkable series of confrontations with the United Kingdom known as the Cod Wars. In the First Cod War of 1958 Iceland extended its fishery limit from four to twelve nautical miles; in the Second, from 1972 to 1973, it pushed the limit out to fifty miles; and in the Third, from 1975 to 1976, to two hundred. Against the Royal Navy's frigates the Icelandic Coast Guard deployed only a handful of patrol vessels, but it used trawl-cutters to sever British nets and, decisively, Reykjavík threatened to leave NATO and close the American base at Keflavík, an alliance asset Washington could not afford to lose in the North Atlantic.

The strategy worked. Each time, Britain ultimately conceded: it acknowledged the twelve-mile limit in 1958, accepted an interim deal in 1973, and in an agreement of 2 June 1976 recognised Iceland's two-hundred-mile zone. The Cod Wars were among the few cases in which a tiny, militarily negligible state imposed its will on a great naval power, and they had consequences far beyond the North Atlantic: Iceland's successful assertion of a two-hundred-mile limit helped establish the principle of the Exclusive Economic Zone, later enshrined in the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea.

In the following decades Iceland became one of the world's most prosperous societies, electing in Vigdís Finnbogadóttir (1980) the world's first popularly elected woman head of state and building a modern welfare state. Prosperity bred a reckless financial boom: by 2008 the assets of its three big banks, Landsbanki, Kaupthing and Glitnir, had swollen to many times the national economy, and when global credit froze the banks collapsed within days in October 2008, the largest banking failure relative to the size of an economy in history. The government passed emergency legislation, the krona crashed and Iceland turned to the International Monetary Fund, but the country recovered within a few years. It remains a republic shaped by its position on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where the North American and Eurasian plates drift apart and recurrent eruptions continue to test the descendants of the settlers.

events.title: First Cod War (12 nautical miles)
events.summary: Iceland extended its fishery limit from four to twelve nautical miles in 1958; after confrontations at sea Britain acknowledged the twelve-mile limit, the first of Iceland's victories over the Royal Navy.

events.title: Second Cod War (50 nautical miles)
events.summary: Iceland extended its limit to fifty miles from September 1972; its coast guard cut dozens of British trawl nets before a NATO-mediated interim agreement was reached in November 1973.

events.title: Third Cod War (200 nautical miles)
events.summary: Iceland claimed a 200-mile zone in 1975; after clashes and a threat to close the Keflavík NATO base, Britain recognised the 200-mile limit in an agreement of 2 June 1976.

events.title: The 200-mile limit and the EEZ
events.summary: Iceland's successful assertion of a 200-mile fishing zone helped establish the Exclusive Economic Zone in international law, later codified in the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea.

events.title: Collapse of the banks
events.summary: The three big Icelandic banks, whose assets dwarfed the economy, failed within days in October 2008 in the largest banking collapse relative to GDP in history; the state passed emergency laws and turned to the IMF before recovering.

figures.name: Vigdís Finnbogadóttir
figures.blurb: Elected president in 1980, Vigdís Finnbogadóttir was the world's first popularly elected woman head of state, serving until 1996 and becoming an enduring symbol of modern Iceland.

figures.name: Geir Hallgrímsson
figures.role: Prime Minister during the Third Cod War
figures.blurb: Prime minister from 1974 to 1978, he led Iceland through the Third Cod War and the brinkmanship over the Keflavík base that secured British recognition of the 200-mile limit in 1976.

pullquote.text: Iceland extended its fishing limits to 12, then 50, then 200 nautical miles, and its victory in the Cod Wars helped establish the 200-mile Exclusive Economic Zone in international law.

figures.name: Ingólfr Arnarson
figures.blurb: By the testimony of the Landnámabók, the first settler to make a lasting home in Iceland, around 874, at the site he named Reykjavík. He is regarded as the founder of the Icelandic nation.

figures.name: Snorri Sturluson
figures.blurb: Chieftain and lawspeaker whose Prose Edda and Heimskringla preserved the mythology and royal history of the Norse world, the supreme figure of Iceland's saga age.

figures.name: Jón Sigurðsson
figures.blurb: Iceland's national hero, who led the nineteenth-century campaign for self-government and the abolition of the Danish monopoly; the republic was founded on his birthday, 17 June.

figures.name: Sveinn Björnsson
figures.blurb: Diplomat and statesman who became the first president of the Republic of Iceland at its founding at Þingvellir on 17 June 1944.

sources.label: The Discovery and Settlement of Iceland
History of Þingvellir and the Alþing
History of Iceland, 1840s to the Second World War
Foreign Relations of the United States, 1949, Vol. IV (Iceland and the North Atlantic Treaty)
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian
